ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for garlic chili sauce; closest defensible match is hot sauce (FOODON_03301710). Garlic chili sauce is a blended condiment of chilies, garlic, ginger, vinegar, sugar, and salt.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Chilli sauce) is the closest defensible match. Garlic chili sauce is a subcategory with additional garlic and ginger components.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No defensible SNOMED CT code identified for garlic chili sauce as a food condiment.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of garlic chili sauce is approximately 15 ml (about 1 tablespoon, roughly 15 g), used as a dipping sauce or condiment alongside Hainanese chicken rice or other poached meats and seafood.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chili sauce, hot, from red chili peppers (SR Legacy) and component ingredient data for garlic, ginger, vinegar, and sugar
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Singaporean-style garlic chili sauce exists in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database. Values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for chili sauce (FDC ID 170163) and component ingredients (garlic, ginger, red chili peppers, vinegar, sugar, salt), cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Foodo nutrient data for similar chili condiments. Sodium value of 1850 mg/100g reflects the high salt content typical of this condiment style. All values are plausible for a blended raw chili-garlic condiment with added salt.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of chili-based condiments and component ingredients — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for chili sauce and vinegar-based condiments
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singaporean garlic chili sauce. GI estimated at 15 (low) based on University of Sydney GI Database data for chili sauce and vinegar-based condiments, which consistently show low GI values due to high acidity, capsaicin content, and low net carbohydrate density. Glycemic load per 15 g serving = (15 × 14.5 / 100) × (15/100) ≈ 0.33, rounded to 0.3. The small amount of added sugar is minimal per serving and does not materially raise the GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (1850 mg per 100 g; approximately 278 mg per 15 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should strictly limit garlic chili sauce to no more than one teaspoon (5 g) per meal, as even a standard 15 g serving contributes approximately 278 mg sodium, which is a significant proportion of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (estimated GI 15) and low glycemic load per serving (GL ≈ 0.3)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Garlic chili sauce is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in typical condiment quantities, as its very low glycemic load (approximately 0.3 per 15 g serving) poses negligible impact on postprandial blood glucose; however, sodium content should be monitored if the patient also has hypertension.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium content offset by negligible saturated fat and beneficial bioactive compounds (capsaicin, allicin)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should use garlic chili sauce sparingly due to its high sodium content (1850 mg/100 g), which can elevate blood pressure; the negligible saturated fat and the presence of capsaicin and allicin may offer modest cardioprotective benefits when consumed in small amounts.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory bioactives (capsaicin, allicin, gingerols) versus pro-inflammatory potential of high sodium intake
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Garlic, ginger, and chili contain well-documented anti-inflammatory compounds; however, the high sodium content of this sauce may be pro-inflammatory in some individuals with rheumatoid arthritis, so use should be limited to small amounts.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Garlic Chili Sauce (蒜蓉辣椒酱, Suànróng làjiāojiàng) is a vibrant, fiery, and tangy condiment that is an essential component of Singaporean and Southeast Asian Chinese cuisine, most famously served alongside Hainanese Chicken Rice. It is prepared by blending fresh red chilies (typically a combination of bird's eye chilies for intense heat and larger red chilies for color and body) with generous quantities of fresh garlic, fresh ginger, rice vinegar, sugar, and salt, with some versions incorporating a splash of lime juice for additional brightness. The sauce is not cooked — it is a raw blended condiment, which preserves the sharp, pungent aroma of fresh garlic and the bright heat of the chilies. Its texture is slightly watery but pulpy, and its color is a vivid orange-red. Nutritionally, it is low in calories (approximately 65 kcal per 100 g) and fat (0.5 g per 100 g), with a moderate carbohydrate content (14.5 g per 100 g) largely from the natural sugars in chilies and added sugar. It is a notable source of vitamin C (85 mg per 100 g) and vitamin A (180 mcg RAE per 100 g) from the chili peppers. Its primary clinical concern is its very high sodium content (approximately 1850 mg per 100 g), which makes it unsuitable for unrestricted use by individuals on low-sodium diets for hypertension or cardiovascular disease management. Its glycemic index is estimated to be low (approximately 15), making it suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in typical condiment portions. The bioactive compounds capsaicin (from chilies), allicin (from garlic), and gingerols (from ginger) contribute anti-inflammatory and potential metabolic benefits. It is naturally vegan, vegetarian, gluten-free, and dairy-free.
common_user_queries: What sauce goes with Hainanese chicken rice?
How many calories in garlic chili sauce?
Is garlic chili sauce suitable for diabetics?
Can I eat garlic chili sauce if I have high blood pressure?
How is Singaporean chili sauce made?
What is the sodium content of garlic chili sauce?
meal_context: Served as a mandatory accompaniment to Hainanese Chicken Rice
Used as a dipping sauce for poached meats and seafood
Used as a condiment for dim sum
Used as a flavor booster for savory rice and noodle dishes
Suitable for lunch and dinner as a condiment

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Singaporean-style garlic chili sauce exists in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy chili sauce data and component ingredient profiles, cross-referenced with HPB Foodo data for similar condiments. The sodium value is consistent with the high-salt formulation typical of this condiment. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database data for chili-based condiments. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the absence of a direct approved-source match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170163, chili sauce) and component ingredient data for garlic, ginger, red chili peppers, vinegar, and sugar, cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Foodo data for similar chili condiments. No exact approved-source entry for Singaporean garlic chili sauce was found. 2. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0: confirmed zero — this is a plant-based condiment containing no animal-derived ingredients; vitamin B12 is naturally zero. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero for a raw blended plant-based condiment with no sun-exposed mushrooms or fortified ingredients. trans_fat_g is 0.0: naturally zero — no hydrogenated fats are used. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: naturally zero — this is a vegan plant-based condiment. 3. water_g set to -1: detailed moisture data for this specific garlic chili sauce formulation is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2026-04-16. 4. Saturated fat (0.1 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (0.05 g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.2 g) are estimated from USDA component ingredient data; these values are plausible for a low-fat blended chili-garlic condiment. 5. GI estimated at 15 (low) based on analogous University of Sydney GI Database entries for chili sauce and vinegar-based condiments. Glycemic load per 15 g serving ≈ 0.3. 6. The duplicate portion entry in the input (two identical '1 serving (15ml)' entries) has been deduplicated to a single 15 g serving. 7. FoodOn match is approximate (hot sauce, FOODON_03301710); FoodEx2 match is approximate (A00LC, Chilli sauce). No SNOMED CT code was identified.
